name: Rhodium
name_origin: From the Greek 'rhodon' meaning 'rose', after the rose-red color of dilute solutions of its chloride salts.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Rhodium is one of the rarest and most valuable elements in Earth's crust at about 0.0002 ppm. It occurs in trace amounts in platinum and nickel ores; about 80% of world supply comes from South Africa, with the remainder from Russia and Canada.

compounds.name: Rhodium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: A red-brown solid that is the most common starting material for rhodium catalysts and complexes.

compounds.name: Rhodium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: A gray solid used as a precursor for rhodium metal and in some catalytic applications.

compounds.name: Wilkinson's catalyst
compounds.summary: A homogeneous catalyst used for hydrogenation of alkenes under mild conditions.

compounds.name: Rhodium hexadecacarbonyl
compounds.summary: A black cluster compound used as a precursor for heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysis.

compounds.name: Rhodium(III) nitrate
compounds.summary: A water-soluble salt used to deposit rhodium coatings and as a catalyst precursor.

uses: Three-way catalytic converters for automobile exhaust (reducing NOx emissions)
Hydrogenation and hydroformylation catalysts in industrial chemistry
Hard, bright, corrosion-resistant electroplating finish for jewelry and mirrors
Electrical contacts and crucibles in laboratory equipment
reactivity_summary: Rhodium is an extremely corrosion-resistant, lustrous silvery-white platinum-group metal. It is unattacked by air or water at room temperature and is insoluble in most acids, including aqua regia in massive form (powdered rhodium dissolves in hot aqua regia and concentrated sulfuric acid). It reacts only slowly with halogens at high temperatures, but molten alkali fusion will dissolve it.
isotopes_summary: Rhodium has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope, 103Rh (100% natural abundance), making it monoisotopic. Roughly 30 radioisotopes have been characterized, the longest-lived being 101Rh (half-life 3.3 years) and 102mRh (half-life 3.7 years). Most rhodium radioisotopes have very short half-lives.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
